perturbation.original_text: 6.3. Subject to 6. 5, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, during the existence of this Agreement disclose the Confidential Information to any Third Party for any reason or purpose whatsoever, without the prior wr itten consent of the Disclosing Party and only to the extent of such consent, save in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement. In this Agreement "Third Party" means any party who is not a signatory to this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3. Subject to 6.5, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, during the existence of this Agreement disclose the Confidential Information to any Third Party for any reason or purpose whatsoever, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and only to the extent of such consent, save in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement. In this Agreement "Third Party" means any party who is not a signatory to this agreement, unless the Third Party is an affiliate of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by creating an exception to the definition of "Third Party." The original definition excludes only signatories, but the modified text adds "unless the Third Party is an affiliate of the Receiving Party." This contradicts section 6.5, which requires Representatives (including subsidiaries/associated companies which could be affiliates) to sign a confidentiality undertaking, implying they are treated as third parties. This creates uncertainty about whether affiliates are bound by the same restrictions as other third parties, leading to disputes over disclosure of Confidential Information. Specifically, does section 6.5 still apply to affiliates now? This change affects which entities the Receiving Party must get consent from before disclosing Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.5. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in this Agreement, the Parties agree that the Confidential Information may be disclosed by the Receiving Party to its respective employees, agents, officer s, directors, subsidiaries, associated companies, shareholders and advisers (including but not limited to professional financial advisers, legal advisers and auditors) ("Representatives") on a need -to- know basis and for the purposes of the Project, pr ovide d that the Receiving Party, prior to making any disclosure of Confidential Information as permitted under this 6.5, procure s that the proposed recipient of such Confidential Information is made aware of the terms of this Agreement and the Receiving Party w ill procure that each such proposed recipient adheres to those terms as if they were a party to this Agreement to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information to Third Parties. The Receiving Party will procure that such proposed reci pient will sign a written confidentiality undertaking prior to such disclosure on terms no less onerous than this Agreement and to the extent that such rights inure to the benefit of the Disclosing Party. For the purposes of this clause, the Receiving Party's Representatives shall be deemed to be acting, in the event of a breach, as the Receiving Party's duly authorized agents.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "Third Party" to exclude affiliates of the Receiving Party. This contradicts section 6.5, which requires the Receiving Party to ensure that its subsidiaries and associated companies (which can be affiliates) sign a confidentiality undertaking as if they were a party to the agreement. This implies they are to be treated as third parties for the purposes of protecting confidential information. The change creates a contradiction because it is now unclear whether affiliates are subject to the same confidentiality obligations as other third parties, specifically whether 6.5 still applies, leading to potential disputes over disclosure of Confidential Information. Therefore, the changed text creates a direct conflict with the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 8. RESTRICTION ON DISCL OSURE AND USE OF CON FIDENTIAL INFORMATION The Rec eiving Party undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than: 8.1. the Project; and 8.2. in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. RESTRICTION ON DISCL OSURE AND USE OF CON FIDENTIAL INFORMATION The Rec eiving Party undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than: 8.1. the Project; 8.2. internal research and development; and 8.3. in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a contradiction by allowing the Receiving Party to use the Confidential Information for "internal research and development" in addition to the Project. This conflicts with the original intent of the agreement, which restricts use to the Project only as stated in Section 6.6: "Except as otherwise contemplated in this Agreement, the Parties agree in favour of one another not to utilize, exploit or in any o ther manner whatsoever use the Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement for any purpose whatsoever other than the Project without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party." The added clause creates uncertainty regarding the scope of permitted use and could lead to disputes if the Disclosing Party believes the research and development goes beyond the intended scope of the project.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.6. Except as otherwise contemplated in this Agreement, the Parties agree in favour of one another not to utilize, exploit or in any o ther manner whatsoever use the Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement for any purpose whatsoever other than the Project without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds "internal research and development" as an acceptable use of confidential information, which directly contradicts section 6.6. Section 6.6 explicitly states that confidential information should only be used for "the Project" unless otherwise specified in the agreement. The added clause in the changed text is not "otherwise specified" elsewhere, thus a clear and direct contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: 10.3. The Receiving Party shall comply with any request by the Disclosing Party in terms of this clause, within 7 (s even) business days of receipt of any such request.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.3. The Receiving Party shall comply with any request by the Disclosing Party in terms of this clause, within 7 (seven) business days of receipt of any such request, unless otherwise agreed upon by both parties in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds "unless otherwise agreed upon by both parties in writing" to the deadline for returning or destroying confidential information. While seemingly innocuous, it creates a contradiction with Section 14, which outlines breach and remedy. Section 14 states that if a breach occurs, the Receiving Party has 7 business days to remedy it, or the Disclosing Party can pursue legal action. This added clause in 10.3 gives the Receiving Party an avenue to extend the deadline for compliance indefinitely by simply claiming an agreement was made, even if it undermines the original intent of a swift remedy as contemplated in Section 14. This makes the 7-day remedy period in Section 14 uncertain and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14. BREACH In the event that the Receiving Party should breach any of the provisions of this Agreement and f ail to remedy such breach within 7 (seven) business days from date of a written notice to do so, then the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to invoke all remedies available to it in law including, but not limited to, the institution of urgent proceedings as well as any other way of relief appropriate under the circumstances, in any court of competent jurisdiction, in the event of breach or threatened breach of the Agreement and/or an action for damages.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 10.3 introduces a loophole by allowing parties to agree to different deadlines in writing, which directly contradicts the fixed 7-day remedy period in section 14. This undermines the enforceability of the 7-day remedy in section 14, as the Receiving Party can indefinitely extend the deadline, making the two sections incompatible. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where different sections provide conflicting deadlines or obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 12. TERM 12.1. This Agreement shall be binding on the Parties with effect from the date of signature of the Party signing last hereto . 12.2. This Agreement shall commence upon the date referred to in clause 12.1 and shall endure for the duration of the Project . Termination shall not, however, affect the rights and obligations contained herein with respect to Confidential Information furnished by one Party to another Party prior to termination .
perturbation.changed_text: 12. TERM 12.1. This Agreement shall be binding on the Parties with effect from the date of signature of the Party signing last hereto . 12.2. This Agreement shall commence upon the date referred to in clause 12.1 and shall endure for the duration of the Project, but no longer than 5 years. Termination shall not, however, affect the rights and obligations contained herein with respect to Confidential Information furnished by one Party to another Party prior to termination .
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction to the contract's term. While the original text states the agreement lasts for the "duration of the Project", the change limits this term to "no longer than 5 years." This creates uncertainty if the Project extends beyond the 5-year period, this is where in text contradiction is taking place, thus, the contract will contradict itself. Does the agreement terminate after 5 years, regardless of project status, or does it continue until project completion, as initially intended? This directly contradicts the original clause defining the agreement's duration.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. TERM 12.1. This Agreement shall be binding on the Parties with effect from the date of signature of the Party signing last hereto . 12.2. This Agreement shall commence upon the date referred to in clause 12.1 and shall endure for the duration of the Project, but no longer than 5 years. Termination shall not, however, affect the rights and obligations contained herein with respect to Confidential Information furnished by one Party to another Party prior to termination .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause 12.2 to limit the agreement's duration to 'no longer than 5 years,' while the contradicted text, which is the same clause, initially stated the agreement would 'endure for the duration of the Project.' This creates a direct conflict as it introduces a specific time limit that may not align with the project's actual duration, as outlined in the explanation provided. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type, where different sections (in this case, parts of the same section) provide conflicting deadlines or obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 15. AMENDMENTS No amendment, interpretation or waiver of any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be effective unless reduced in writing and signed by both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. AMENDMENTS No amendment, interpretation or waiver of any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be effective unless reduced in writing and signed by both Parties. However, Eskom reserves the right to unilaterally amend this agreement with 30 days written notice to the Counterparty.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by stating that while amendments generally require written agreement from both parties, Eskom can unilaterally amend the agreement with 30 days' notice. This contradicts the initial requirement for mutual agreement and raises questions about the enforceability of previous clauses. For example, if Eskom unilaterally amends a clause related to Confidential Information, does it supersede the previously agreed-upon restrictions, potentially negating the original intention of those agreements? This affects the overall balance of power and could lead to disputes over which version of the agreement is legally binding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15. AMENDMENTS No amendment, interpretation or waiver of any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be effective unless reduced in writing and signed by both Parties. However, Eskom reserves the right to unilaterally amend this agreement with 30 days written notice to the Counterparty.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 15 introduces a direct contradiction. The original text states that all amendments require written agreement from both parties. The changed text adds a clause allowing Eskom to unilaterally amend the agreement with 30 days' notice. This directly contradicts the initial requirement for mutual agreement, creating an inconsistency within the same section. The Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text, because the contradiction appears within the same section.

perturbation.original_text: 17.2. The Disclosing Party warrants that disclosure of the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party: 17.2.1. will not result in a breach of any other agreement to which it is a party; and 17.2.2. will not, to the best of its knowledge and belief, infringe the rights of any Third Party, and the Disclosing Party hereby indemnifies and holds the Rece iving Party harmless against any liability for Third Party claims on such a basis.
perturbation.changed_text: 17.2. The Disclosing Party warrants that disclosure of the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party: 17.2.1. will not result in a breach of any other agreement to which it is a party; 17.2.2. will not, to the best of its knowledge and belief, infringe the rights of any Third Party, and the Disclosing Party hereby indemnifies and holds the Rece iving Party harmless against any liability for Third Party claims on such a basis; except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by adding an exception to the Disclosing Party's indemnity for Third Party claims. The original text states that the Disclosing Party will indemnify the Receiving Party against any liability for Third Party claims related to infringement. The added clause removes this indemnity if the Receiving Party exhibits gross negligence or willful misconduct. This contradicts the overall intention to protect the Receiving Party, as it shifts liability under certain circumstances. It contradicts the absolute nature of the original indemnity clause and creates uncertainty about when the Receiving Party is actually protected from Third Party claims. The added exception clause affects the risk assessment by the Receiving Party, as they are now exposed to liability they initially believed they were shielded from, affecting the overall allocation of risks.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 17.2. The Disclosing Party warrants that disclosure of the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party: 17.2.1. will not result in a breach of any other agreement to which it is a party; 17.2.2. will not, to the best of its knowledge and belief, infringe the rights of any Third Party, and the Disclosing Party hereby indemnifies and holds the Rece iving Party harmless against any liability for Third Party claims on such a basis; except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to the Disclosing Party's indemnity, stating they are not liable for Third Party claims if the Receiving Party exhibits gross negligence or willful misconduct. The original text provides a warranty that the Disclosing Party indemnifies and holds the Receiving Party harmless against any liability for Third Party claims. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text and so it has the added except clause, which contradicts the absolute nature of the original indemnity clause. Since the original text does not have this exception, the changed text and contradicted text now contradict the idea of complete indemnification, creating a clear in-text contradiction as described by 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.
